content_en:
The science of hadith defects ('ilal al-hadith) addresses the hidden flaws that can compromise an apparently sound narration without being immediately visible to anyone but the most expert critics. Ahmad ibn Hanbal's Al-Ilal wa Marifat al-Rijal provides an extensive practical catalogue of such defects, drawn from his evaluation of specific narrations rather than from abstract theoretical taxonomy. The categories he identifies and illustrates in the work became the standard framework for subsequent discussions of hadith defects in the classical literature.

The mursal narration presents one of the most common and consequential defect types. A mursal hadith is one in which a successor (tabi'i) transmits directly from the Prophet, peace be upon him, without naming the companion through whom he received the narration. Since the successors did not themselves meet the Prophet, a mursal chain has a gap at its most critical link. Ahmad treated mursal narrations with great caution, accepting them only under specific conditions and noting carefully when a scholar's reputation for mursal narration made his chains structurally weaker than they appeared. Related to this is the munqati' (broken) chain, in which a gap or interruption occurs at any point other than the companion link.

Tadlis, the concealment of a weak link in a chain, was for Ahmad among the most serious defects because it involves an element of deliberate misleading. A mudallis narrator omits a weak intermediary and phrases his transmission in a way that suggests direct reception, creating the false impression of a sound uninterrupted chain. Ahmad was meticulous in identifying narrators known for this practice, categorizing them by the frequency and seriousness of their tadlis and advising scholars on how to assess their narrations accordingly. Irsaal khafi, a subtler form in which a narrator reports from a contemporary he never actually met, is closely related and equally problematic.

The shadhdh (irregular) narration occurs when a reliable narrator reports something that contradicts what more numerous or more reliable narrators report from the same source. The ma'lul (subtly defective) narration is a broader category encompassing narrations that appear sound but have some hidden flaw detectable only through comparative analysis of multiple chains. Ahmad's practice of collecting numerous chains for a single hadith before issuing his judgment on it exemplifies the method required to identify these defects: only by mapping the entire transmission history of a narration can the expert critic spot the anomaly that reveals its true status. These categories, drawn from Ahmad's practical work, constitute the essential vocabulary of hadith criticism.
content_id:
Ilmu cacat-cacat hadis (ilal al-hadis) membahas kekurangan-kekurangan tersembunyi yang dapat mengkompromikan sebuah riwayat yang tampaknya sahih tanpa langsung terlihat oleh siapa pun kecuali para kritikus yang paling ahli. Al-Ilal wa Ma'rifat al-Rijal karya Ahmad ibn Hanbal menyediakan katalog praktis yang ekstensif dari cacat-cacat semacam itu, yang diambil dari penilaiannya atas riwayat-riwayat spesifik daripada dari taksonomi teoretis abstrak. Kategori-kategori yang ia identifikasi dan ilustrasikan dalam karya ini menjadi kerangka standar untuk pembahasan-pembahasan selanjutnya tentang cacat-cacat hadis dalam literatur klasik.

Hadis mursal menyajikan salah satu jenis cacat yang paling umum dan paling berpengaruh. Hadis mursal adalah hadis di mana seorang tabi'in meriwayatkan langsung dari Nabi ﷺ tanpa menyebutkan nama sahabat melalui siapa ia menerima riwayat tersebut. Karena para tabi'in tidak bertemu sendiri dengan Nabi, sebuah sanad mursal memiliki celah pada mata rantainya yang paling kritis. Ahmad memperlakukan riwayat-riwayat mursal dengan sangat hati-hati, menerimanya hanya dalam kondisi-kondisi tertentu dan mencatat dengan cermat ketika reputasi seorang ulama dalam periwayatan mursal membuat sanad-sanadnya secara struktural lebih lemah dari yang tampak. Terkait dengan ini adalah sanad munqathi' (terputus), di mana celah atau gangguan terjadi di titik mana pun selain mata rantai sahabat.

Tadlis, penyembunyian mata rantai yang lemah dalam sebuah sanad, bagi Ahmad termasuk cacat yang paling serius karena melibatkan unsur penyesatan yang disengaja. Seorang perawi mudallis menghilangkan perantara yang lemah dan mengungkapkan periwayatannya dengan cara yang menyiratkan penerimaan langsung, menciptakan kesan palsu tentang sanad yang sahih dan tidak terputus. Ahmad sangat teliti dalam mengidentifikasi perawi-perawi yang dikenal dengan praktik ini, mengkategorikan mereka berdasarkan frekuensi dan keseriusan tadlis mereka serta memberikan saran kepada para ulama tentang cara menilai riwayat-riwayat mereka sesuai dengan itu. Irsaal khafi, sebuah bentuk yang lebih halus di mana seorang perawi meriwayatkan dari seorang sezaman yang tidak pernah benar-benar ia jumpai, sangat terkait dan sama-sama bermasalah.

Hadis syaadz (tidak biasa) terjadi ketika seorang perawi andal meriwayatkan sesuatu yang bertentangan dengan apa yang diriwayatkan oleh perawi-perawi yang lebih banyak atau lebih andal dari sumber yang sama. Hadis ma'lul (cacat secara halus) adalah kategori yang lebih luas yang mencakup riwayat-riwayat yang tampak sahih tetapi memiliki beberapa kekurangan tersembunyi yang hanya dapat dideteksi melalui analisis komparatif beberapa sanad. Praktik Ahmad dalam mengumpulkan sejumlah besar sanad untuk satu hadis sebelum mengeluarkan penilaiannya mencontohkan metode yang diperlukan untuk mengidentifikasi cacat-cacat ini: hanya dengan memetakan seluruh sejarah transmisi sebuah riwayat, sang kritikus ahli dapat menemukan anomali yang mengungkapkan status sebenarnya. Kategori-kategori ini, yang diambil dari pekerjaan praktis Ahmad, merupakan kosakata esensial kritik hadis.
content_ar:
The science of hadith defects ('ilal al-hadith) addresses the hidden flaws that can compromise an apparently sound narration without being immediately visible to anyone but the most expert critics. Ahmad ibn Hanbal's Al-Ilal wa Marifat al-Rijal provides an extensive practical catalogue of such defects, drawn from his evaluation of specific narrations rather than from abstract theoretical taxonomy. The categories he identifies and illustrates in the work became the standard framework for subsequent discussions of hadith defects in the classical literature.

The mursal narration presents one of the most common and consequential defect types. A mursal hadith is one in which a successor (tabi'i) transmits directly from النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم, peace be upon him, without naming the companion through whom he received the narration. Since the successors did not themselves meet النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم, a mursal chain has a gap at its most critical link. Ahmad treated mursal narrations with great caution, accepting them only under specific conditions and noting carefully when a scholar's reputation for mursal narration made his chains structurally weaker than they appeared. Related to this is the munqati' (broken) chain, in which a gap or interruption occurs at any point other than the companion link.

Tadlis, the concealment of a weak link in a chain, was for Ahmad among the most serious defects because it involves an element of deliberate misleading. A mudallis narrator omits a weak intermediary and phrases his transmission in a way that suggests direct reception, creating the false impression of a sound uninterrupted chain. Ahmad was meticulous in identifying narrators known for this practice, categorizing them by the frequency and seriousness of their tadlis and advising scholars on how to assess their narrations accordingly. Irsaal khafi, a subtler form in which a narrator reports from a contemporary he never actually met, is closely related and equally problematic.

The shadhdh (irregular) narration occurs when a reliable narrator reports something that contradicts what more numerous or more reliable narrators report from the same source. The ma'lul (subtly defective) narration is a broader category encompassing narrations that appear sound but have some hidden flaw detectable only through comparative analysis of multiple chains. Ahmad's practice of collecting numerous chains for a single hadith before issuing his judgment on it exemplifies the method required to identify these defects: only by mapping the entire transmission history of a narration can the expert critic spot the anomaly that reveals its true status. These categories, drawn from Ahmad's practical work, constitute the essential vocabulary of hadith criticism.
